data.title: CGDV: The More Flexible Strategy Should Be Key In A More Challenging Economic Environment
data.description:
Investing has gotten harder in 2026. While basic index investing strategies produced historically strong results for most of the last decade, a more mixed current growth picture, with prices higher and more middle- to low-income consumers struggling, most updated economic estimates suggest that those kinds of unique returns will be harder to come by moving forward.

With institutions such as the IMF forecasting global growth to be at 3%-3.5% in 2026, Europe continuing to face economic weakness, and China entering a new phase of more moderate and slow expansion, investments with more nuanced investing strategies are looking more likely to produce better financial results.

One ETF that has successfully used this kind of flexible approach to continue to consistently outperform the broader indexes is the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV).

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has offered investors total returns of 89.39% over the last 3 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 75.11 percent. CGDV has outperformed the S&P 500 on a 1-year, 3-year, and 5-year timeframe.

I last wrote about the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF in April of 2025, and I rated the fund a buy. I am upgrading this investment to a strong buy today. CGDV. Recent economic data shows a mixed growth picture, some stubbornly high inflation metrics suggest aggressive rate cuts are increasingly unlikely, and geopolitical uncertainty remains very high. This fund's more targeted approach that focuses on both stronger sectors and better-performing individual companies should continue to offer investors better value than many simpler, more broad-based investing strategies focused on the indexes.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has an expense ratio of 0.33%, a current yield of 1.26%, and $30.52 billion in assets under management. The fund is invested 26.53% in technology, 15.37% in the industrials, 13.11% in health care, 8.85% in consumer cyclicals, 6.58% in consumer defensives, 6.34% in energy, 5.51% in communication, 5.15% in financials, 4.90% in basic materials, 2.67% in the utilities, and 1.74% in real estate. The four largest holdings of CGDV are Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), RTX Corporation (RTX), and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). These positions make up a combined nearly 19% of this investment.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF officially reconstitutes twice a year and rebalances periodically at the fund's discretion, but this investment also, at times, changes holdings throughout the year. CGDV's fund managers have significant flexibility in deciding what positions to have and how to adjust the percentage of the portfolio these investments comprise. No single equity holding of CGDV is more than 5.34%; this is a well-diversified fund.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF seeks to pick high-quality companies with a history of strong cash flow, and the investment allocates capital 90% to US equities, with nearly all the holdings being large-cap corporations.

While CGDV's prospectus claims the fund targets funds with an average dividend yield higher than the average US stock, this ETF has never had a full-year yield of over 2%. This is a growth investment.

CGDV has outperformed both the S&P 500 and most of the fund's peers primarily because of the fund's overweight position in large-cap and also because of the investment's focus on higher-quality companies with stronger cash flow.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF also takes a markedly different approach to capital allocation than other funds with significant tech holdings, such as the Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) and the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO). Since this investment limits holdings to at or near 5 percent of the exchange-traded fund's overall portfolio in addition to focusing on higher-quality names within this sector. CGDV did not hold Oracle Corporation (ORCL), one of the worst-performing large-cap tech companies over the last year; the fund's more nuanced selection process led the ETF's managers to avoid the software operator. CGDV has far less concentrated holdings in the tech sector than most peers, and the ETF's focus on cash flow and the recent performance of management teams has worked. With the growth picture more uncertain and several tech companies, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Oracle, recently missing earnings expectations by a significant margin, CGDV's strategy of having a more diversified portfolio within industries should produce better results moving forward as well.

CGDV's strategy of focusing on dividends and cash flow is also why the ETF has held some of the best-performing companies in the market over the last several years, such as General Electric Company (GE), Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), and British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI), and Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL). Corporations such as General Electric and Philip Morris International have been two of the best-performing corporations in the market over the last 2 years, and this ETF has consistently done a good job selecting some of the strongest-performing industries and companies in the market. General Electric and Philip Morris are both industry leaders that have significantly outperformed the market in the last several years. General Electric is in the middle of an extended secular growth market in the aerospace industry, while Philip Morris International continues to dominate the fast-growing and higher-margin alternative smoking industry. Royal Caribbean also remains the premium operator in the cruise industry, and the company is well-positioned to benefit from recent economic reports showing spending continues to hold up very well at the top end, since the cruise operator focuses on luxury vacation offerings. CGDV's dividend-based approach of targeting companies continuing to generate impressive cash flow even in the more challenging current financial environment should keep this ETF focused on both the best-run and stronger-performing companies in an economic situation where some corporations are likely to struggle.

This Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has even offered investors better returns over the last 5 years than even some of the better-performing large-cap funds, such as the Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF ((SCHG)). The investment has also recently outperformed the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) as well.

CGDV has outperformed SCHG by nearly 16% since this fund's inception in February of 2022. The Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, so this investment does not use the same discretion that CGDV does when picking companies.

CGDV has also significantly outperformed SPMO more recently, over the last six months, even though this investment has performed better than the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF over the last 4 years.

While the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF focuses on total returns and momentum, this investment is still a large-cap growth fund that holds a number of the same core holdings as CGDV, such as Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), General Electric, and Broadcom.

SPMO uses a specific formula to calculate momentum and rebalance the fund's portfolio semi-annually that attempts to identify companies most likely to outperform from the S&P 500 momentum index. While SPMO performed very well over the long term, CGDV was able to avoid some struggling equity holdings that the fund's Invesco peer held, such as Oracle (ORCL), since this ETF uses a more nuanced approach to targeting investments. This is also why this exchange-traded fund has just 3.4 percent of the ETF's holdings in Europe, a region seeing more pronounced economic weakness, while nearly 90.5% of the positions of CGDV are in the US, which remains the strongest major economy. Dividend investing strategies have also worked better more recently than the approach of funds such as SPMO of targeting momentum, primarily because the economic data and earnings reports continue to be more fluid and harder to predict in what is an increasingly uncertain economic environment.

The recent financial data remains mixed, and the market outlook remains uncertain, with geopolitical tensions rising, the possibility of continued trade conflicts still high, and recent inflation data showing the Producer Price Index rose more than expected. The IMF is projecting global growth to be 3.3% in 2026 and 3.2% in 2027. Still, the Fed has seen a recent deterioration in consumer spending by lower-income families, and economic estimates for Europe remain weak, with some analysts forecasting growth in this region to be just above 1 percent. Companies such as McDonald's Corporation (MCD) and Walmart Inc. (WMT) have also seen a recent slowdown in spending at the lower end. The Fed has also observed that spending at the high end has not slowed at all. Top-end spenders made up more of overall consumption in the US at any time than since this data began to be tracked in 1989.

This kind of more complicated financial outlook should give funds such as CGDV that target individual companies that are outperforming in the current, more nuanced economic environment and use more discretion as an advantage. With growth expected to be less broad-based, narrowing the industries and companies most investors are allocating capital to should also make more sense, and The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF's approach of focusing on higher-quality corporations with stronger cash flow is likely to separate this fund's performance from the more formulaic approach many of CGDV's peers are using. This more aggressive growth investment should fit well in portfolios that are seeking to outperform the broader indexes.

All investments have risk, and the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has not had to navigate a prolonged and more severe sell-off since its inception date in February 2022. CGDV's more narrow approach to investing means the fund is less likely to benefit from an improving macro-outlook than more broad-based index investments that have significant positions in some of the weaker-performing industries, such as consumer cyclicals. CGDV also has minimal holdings in the energy and basic materials sectors. This ETF is not likely to be well-positioned right now if inflation were to accelerate significantly in the near term.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF is one of the few investment funds that has consistently and significantly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 4 years, and this investment fund's more narrowly tailored strategy of selecting companies should position this exchange-traded fund well in an economy where growth is increasingly concentrated in sectors such as technology and health care. While index investing may continue to produce strong results, the wider discretion CGDV uses to allocate capital should be an effective approach in the current more challenging market environment.

The consensus growth outlook remains positive despite the more complicated geopolitical and economic environment that is expected by most analysts moving forward. Growth funds such as CGDV should still be able to offer investors consistent solid returns of 8-10% if the current financial outlook of many economists that calls for global growth of nearly 3% this year holds up. While CGDV won't likely be able to offer investors the same historically strong returns seen in the last decade, most investors should still find this investment appealing.
